question: How do the safety features in Table 1.2-2 relate to the DHRS performance shown in Ch.05 Figure 5.4-7 and DHRS-related text?

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the types of alloys and stainless steels used, their compliance with ASME BPVC standards, and the purpose of cladding for corrosion protection and compatibility.

nuscale_ch01_0068.title: Electrical and Instrumentation and Control Drawings
nuscale_ch01_0068.summary: This section describes the inclusion of simplified electrical, instrumentation, and control (I&C) drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It references tables and figures listing and explaining the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide additional site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It references tables and figures that list and explain the symbols and diagrams used, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling methods are unavailable. It operates without external power, ensuring the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, thus maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits. The DHRS's reliability and risk significance are systematically evaluated, and it is classified as Quality Group B per regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design elements provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities of the COL applicant. The section also notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0118.title: 1.8 Interfaces with Certified Design
nuscale_ch01_0118.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified plant portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities. The section also notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0020.title: Safety Considerations
nuscale_ch01_0020.summary: The NuScale design enhances safety by maintaining a vacuum in the containment vessel (CNV), which reduces corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits combustible gases. Its passive safety systems and integral design eliminate the need for external power and large coolant loops, significantly lowering the risk of core damage and improving safety compared to traditional nuclear plants.

nuscale_ch01_0783.title: SRP 15.6.5.B, Rev 1: Radiological Consequences of a Design Basis Loss-of-Coolant Accident: Leakage From Engineered Safety Feature Components Outside ContainmentII.2 Calculation of Radiological Consequences
nuscale_ch01_0783.summary: The section reviews NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for calculating radiological consequences of a design basis loss-of-coolant accident, specifically regarding leakage from engineered safety feature components outside containment. It notes that while some guidance is applicable and partially conformed to, the acceptance criteria and methodologies for radiological analyses are superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3, and certain criteria apply only to BWRs and are not relevant to NuScale. Additionally, NuScale conforms to requirements regarding inadvertent ECCS operation, including maintaining RCS pressure and minimum DNBR.

nuscale_ch01_0666.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.1 Specific CriterionSystem Pressure
nuscale_ch01_0666.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational events such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets requirements for system pressure, maintains critical heat flux above safety limits, ensures anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs) do not escalate to more serious conditions, and uses appropriate instrument setpoints, aligning with the Standard Review Plan and Design Specific Review Standard.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for abnormal operational occurrences such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets specific criteria for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions. The report references compliance with RG 1.105 and the Standard Review Plan requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0022.title: Engineered Safety Feature Materials
nuscale_ch01_0022.summary: The section describes how the NuScale reactor’s containment vessel (CNV) is maintained under vacuum to reduce corrosion, facilitate leak detection, and minimize heat transfer, which enhances safety and reduces debris. The design relies on passive safety features, such as natural cooling and the elimination of large external coolant loops, resulting in a lower core damage frequency and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional reactors.

nuscale_ch01_0784.title: SRP 15.6.5.B, Rev 1: Radiological Consequences of a Design Basis Loss-of-Coolant Accident: Leakage From Engineered Safety Feature Components Outside ContainmentII.3 Combining Radiological Consequences
nuscale_ch01_0784.summary: The section explains that NuScale's Final Safety Analysis Report partially conforms to SRP 15.6.5.B, Rev 1 guidance regarding the calculation and combination of radiological consequences from engineered safety feature (ESF) component leakage outside containment. While the guidance on combining radiological consequences is applicable, the specific acceptance criteria and methodologies are superseded by SRP Section 15.0.3, and some guidance applies only to BWRs, not to NuScale's design.

nuscale_ch01_0413.title: SRP 9.5.7, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Lubrication SystemAll (II.1 thru II.4) Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, and GDC 17
nuscale_ch01_0413.summary: The NuScale plant design does not include or require safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the regulatory criteria outlined in SRP 9.5.7 (and related sections) regarding emergency diesel engine lubrication systems are not applicable. The plant’s safety functions do not rely on either AC or DC power, making these requirements irrelevant to the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0652.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.8.B Sequence of Events and Systems Operation
nuscale_ch01_0652.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analyses, noting areas of full and partial compliance with DSRS 15.0 requirements. Specifically, for Sequence of Events and Systems Operation (I.8.B), NuScale partially conforms, with the exception of Item B.vi, which is only applicable to COL applicants. The table outlines how NuScale addresses each criterion, highlighting any deviations or stricter standards applied.

nuscale_ch01_0591.title: SRP 13.3, Rev 3: Emergency PlanningII.12 Emergency Response Data System
nuscale_ch01_0591.summary: The NuScale design partially conforms to SRP 13.3, Rev 3, Section II.12 regarding the Emergency Response Data System, as it includes such a system in its design. However, site-specific implementation and responsibilities are assigned to the COL (Combined License) applicant referencing the NuScale certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0584.title: SRP 13.3, Rev 3: Emergency PlanningII.5 Upgrading Emergency Response Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0584.summary: The section states that there are no proposed changes to existing emergency response facilities in relation to SRP 13.3, Rev 3: Emergency Planning II.5. All requirements and responsibilities for emergency planning, including facility upgrades, remain with the COL applicant referencing the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0412.title: SRP 9.5.6, Rev 3: Emergency Diesel Engine Starting SystemAll (II.1 thru II.4)Compliance with GDC 2, GDC 4, GDC 5, and GDC 17
nuscale_ch01_0412.summary: The NuScale plant design does not include or require safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore the requirements of SRP 9.5.6 (and related sections) regarding emergency diesel engine starting systems are not applicable. No AC or DC power is relied upon for the performance of NuScale plant safety functions, making compliance with the referenced General Design Criteria (GDC) unnecessary for these systems.

nuscale_ch01_0651.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.8.A Identification of Causes and Frequency Classification
nuscale_ch01_0651.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analyses, noting areas of full and partial compliance with DSRS 15.0 requirements. While most criteria are met, some aspects—such as frequency analysis for AOOs and specific acceptance criteria for IEs and postulated accidents—only partially conform due to differences in analytical approach or stricter internal standards. The identification of causes and frequency classification specifically is noted as conforming to regulatory expectations.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, all of which are closed during normal operation. During an emergency, the system vents steam into containment for condensation and recirculates water to cool the core, with heat ultimately conducted passively to the reactor pool.

nuscale_ch01_0160.title: DSRS 3.11, Rev. 0: Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical EquipmentII.25 Design and Procurement Specifications
nuscale_ch01_0160.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria related to the environmental qualification of mechanical and electrical equipment, as well as the design and procurement specifications for piping systems and threaded fasteners. It notes that certain requirements, such as DSRS 3.11 II.25, are not applicable to NuScale and are instead items for the COL applicant, while other criteria are met as indicated. Additionally, some guidance is marked not applicable because it pertains only to BWR plants.

nuscale_ch01_0727.title: DSRS 15.2.8, Rev 0: Feedwater System Pipe Break Inside and Outside Containment (PWR)II.3 Calculated Site Boundary Doses
nuscale_ch01_0727.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for DSRS 15.2.8, specifically regarding calculated site boundary doses resulting from a feedwater system pipe break inside and outside containment. The analysis confirms that NuScale meets the required standards, with no exceptions noted for this criterion.

nuscale_ch01_0023.title: Containment Systems
nuscale_ch01_0023.summary: The NuScale containment vessel (CNV) is kept under vacuum during normal operation, which reduces moisture and corrosion, aids in leak detection, and limits oxygen for safety. In the event of an emergency, passive cooling rapidly reduces pressure and temperature, and the design’s reliance on passive safety systems and small fuel inventory results in improved safety and a lower core damage frequency compared to conventional reactors.

Section A describes engineered safety feature materials and their role in maintaining containment integrity (e.g., vacuum in CNV), which directly impacts the potential for leakage and thus the radiological consequences calculated and regulated in Section B.

Section A discusses the regulatory compliance and design philosophy that eliminates reliance on AC or DC power for safety functions, which directly supports the DHRS safety features listed in Table 1.2-2 and their passive performance as demonstrated in Section B's DHRS-related figures and text.

Section A outlines the regulatory compliance and design philosophy regarding emergency power systems, stating that no safety-related emergency diesel generators are required or relied upon, while Section B provides the corresponding electrical and I&C drawings that visually clarify this design approach by omitting such systems.

Section A outlines the regulatory framework and event categorization for transient and accident analyses, which sets the context and requirements that the DHRS safety features and performance described in Section B must satisfy.

Section B provides detailed electrical and I&C drawings, including Table 1, which visually clarify the design and operational states of safety features whose performance and operation during transients and accidents are analyzed in Section A.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and safety requirements that the NuScale design must meet, while Section B provides the detailed Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams that visually clarify how the design, including safety features, implements those requirements.

Section A explains that the NuScale design does not rely on AC or DC power or emergency diesel generators for safety functions, which directly supports the DHRS performance described in Section B by demonstrating that the DHRS and related safety features are inherently passive and do not require powered support systems.

Section A explains that the NuScale design does not rely on AC or DC power (such as emergency diesel generators) for safety functions, while Section B provides the detailed drawings (including I&C and electrical diagrams) that visually clarify how these safety features are implemented, thus Section B supports and illustrates the claims made in Section A regarding the independence from external power sources.

Section A discusses the classification and regulatory conformance of transient and accident analyses, which set the context and requirements for evaluating safety features like those in Table 1.2-2, while Section B provides detailed design information about reactor coolant system components (including the pressurizer) that are part of or interact with those safety features, thus specifying the technical basis for the safety analysis.

Section A addresses compliance with safety-related system requirements (e.g., emergency diesel systems) that support plant safety functions, while Section B outlines emergency planning criteria, but both reference the NuScale FSAR's conformance with regulatory criteria, connecting them through the plant's overall safety and emergency preparedness strategy.

Section A details the NuScale plant's compliance with safety-related system requirements (such as not relying on emergency diesel generators), while Section B outlines emergency planning and radiation protection program requirements, with both sections connected through their roles in ensuring overall plant safety and regulatory conformance, particularly as they relate to the design's passive safety features and emergency response capabilities.

Section A and Section B both document that the NuScale plant design does not require or include safety-related emergency diesel generators, indicating that neither the lubrication system (A) nor the starting system (B) is applicable, as no AC or DC power is relied upon for safety functions.

Section A (Sequence of Events and Systems Operation) depends on the identification and frequency classification of causes in Section B to define which events and system responses must be analyzed in the transient and accident analyses.

Section A outlines emergency planning requirements and their applicability, while Section B addresses the classification and analysis of transient and accident events, with both sections connected through the regulatory framework that ensures emergency planning is informed by the identification and categorization of potential accident scenarios.

Section A describes the ECCS safety features relevant to decay heat removal, while Section B lists regulatory requirements (DSRS 3.11) for environmental qualification of equipment, which the ECCS components would need to satisfy to ensure reliable DHRS performance as shown in Figure 5.4-7.

Section A describes the design and function of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), which is one of the safety features listed in Table 1.2-2, while Section B documents the regulatory conformance of the plant's systems (including ECCS and DHRS) with DSRS requirements, thus connecting the design features to their regulatory performance evaluation.

Section A describes the ECCS safety feature that vents steam into the CNV, while Section B details the CNV's vacuum condition and its benefits, showing that the ECCS operation relies on and affects the containment system's state, which in turn influences DHRS performance as shown in the referenced table and figure.

Section A specifies the design and procurement requirements for mechanical and electrical equipment, which are prerequisites for the safety analysis and performance evaluations (such as DHRS performance and site boundary dose calculations) discussed in Section B.

Section A references regulatory requirements and conformance related to equipment qualification and piping analysis, while Section B describes specific containment system features (such as vacuum maintenance) that are safety features likely listed in Table 1.2-2 and relevant to DHRS performance, thus Section B provides detailed design context that Section A's regulatory framework applies to.

Section A references the regulatory conformance of safety features (including DHRS) in response to specific accident scenarios, while Section B describes containment system design features that support the effectiveness and reliability of those safety systems, such as maintaining a vacuum to enhance DHRS performance.
